expectations:
- It's fine (and expected) to mention popular frameworks like Scrum and Kanban as part of the methodologies
      - MUST be in Brazilian Portuguese

      
  - It should assume learners start with no prior knowledge of the subject
  - Should create a progressive learning path going from basic/beginner to mastery of the subject
  - By the end of this course, they should be able to lead very complex projects and tasks in this field
  - They should be prepared for certifications or advanced studies in this subject like a master's degree or PhD
  - Should include **EVERYTHING** a student needs to be at the top 1% of the field
  - Should follow the language specified by language parameter
  - Should follow title and description guidelines: no fluff, be concise, straight to the point
  - Should cover latest trends in the field
  - Prefer concept names over vendor names in chapter titles/descriptions (e.g., "Package management" instead of "npm"). Ecosystem-level tools (npm, yarn, Redux, Zustand) should be avoided in titles
    - Exception: foundational tools that ARE the course subject matter are allowed (e.g., "Git" in a programming course, "Node.js" in a web dev course, "Docker" in a DevOps course). The test: would a chapter about this subject be incomplete without mentioning this tool?
    - Vendor name usage is a minor issue — it affects title quality but not pedagogical content. Do NOT treat it as a major error
  - This is an eval system for a learning platform, of course the definition of "ready for a job" doesn't mean they have the legal requirements to work in the field (eg. medical license, law license, etc.). That's not important here, we're assessing if the course prepares the student with the necessary knowledge and skills.
  - You don't need to evaluate the output format here, just focus on the chapter content quality.
  - Titles should be concise and straight to the point, no fluff/filler words. For example:
    - Just "HTML" is better than "HTML Structure and Semantics" (structure and semantics are implied if this is the only HTML chapter). Similarly, if we only have one CSS chapter, just "CSS" is better than "CSS Styling and Layout".
    - "Front-end frameworks" is better than "Front-End UI Frameworks Overview" (too verbose) or "Front-End Frameworks: Core Concepts" (core concepts is unnecessary fluff)
    - "Relational Databases" is better than "Relational Databases and SQL" (SQL is implied, no need to mention)
    - "Introduction to Java" is better than "Introduction to Java and Setting Up Your Development Environment" (too verbose)
    - "Lean Startup" is better than "The Lean Startup Methodology: An Overview" (too verbose)
    - "Introduction to X" is valid for titles of introductory chapters but we should avoid those words in descriptions
  - Similarly, descriptions should be concise and straight to the point, no fluff/filler words. For example:
    - "Styling with CSS: Selectors, properties, the box model, Flexbox, CSS Grid, and cascade principles." is better than "Master styling and layout with CSS, including selectors, properties, the box model, Flexbox, CSS Grid, and cascade principles." - "Master" is fluff, and so are words like "learn", "understand", "explore", etc.
    - "Properties of matter, states, and phase transitions." is better than "Explore the definition of Chemistry, properties of matter, states, and phase transitions." - "Explore the definition of Chemistry" are filler/unnecessary words.
  - Don't add assessment-style chapters: no final projects, capstone assignments, exercises, quizzes, or "test your knowledge" chapters. A theoretical synthesis chapter (e.g., "Design Patterns in Architecture") is fine even if it synthesizes prior material — the rule targets assessment/project chapters, not synthesis of knowledge. If a chapter uses the word "capstone" but is theoretical (not an assignment), treat it as a minor issue at most, not a major error
  - It shouldn't mention in title or description references to 1% or similar instructions about being top 1%

rankings.reasoning: Excellent comprehensive coverage with 41 chapters progressing logically from foundations to advanced topics. All in Brazilian Portuguese. Covers all major frameworks (Scrum, Kanban, XP, Lean, Crystal, FDD, DSDM), scaling frameworks, product management, technical practices, organizational transformation, career guidance, and trends. Titles are generally concise and professional. Descriptions are direct without excessive fluff. Good separation of concerns (e.g., separate chapters for Kanban principles vs. Kanban metrics, Scrum basics vs. Scrum advanced). Includes career chapter and certifications chapter. Covers compliance, governance, risk, budgeting, distributed teams, and business agility. The 'Estudos de Caso e Anti-Patterns' chapter is not an assessment but a synthesis chapter, which is fine. Minor issues: some titles could be slightly more concise (e.g., 'Agilidade em Escala: SAFe, LeSS, Nexus e Outros' names specific vendors in the title which could be in description instead, and 'Liderança Ágil e Management 3.0' names a specific framework). Overall very strong coverage with appropriate depth.

Very comprehensive with 63 chapters covering an impressive breadth and depth. All in Brazilian Portuguese. Exceptionally detailed progression from fundamentals through advanced topics. Covers Lean thinking, Cynefin, culture, team dynamics, product ownership, product management, strategy/OKRs, discovery, service design, product metrics, backlog, user stories, prioritization, roadmaps, estimation, Scrum (with separate chapters for each event which is very granular), Kanban, Scrumban, XP, regulated contexts, DevOps, SRE, architecture, technical debt, testing, security/DevSecOps, data/analytics, risk, transformation, scaling, portfolio, organizational design, finances, contracts, stakeholders, facilitation, communication, metrics, governance, agile beyond software, hardware, R&D, distributed teams, tools, anti-patterns, coaching, knowledge management, critical thinking, AI, sustainability, ethics, certifications, and career. The granularity is exceptional (e.g., separate chapters for Sprint Review, Daily Scrum, Retrospectives). Some titles are slightly verbose but generally good. The depth is remarkable and would genuinely prepare someone for mastery. Minor concerns: possibly too granular in some areas (e.g., 4 separate Scrum event chapters might be excessive), but this doesn't hurt quality. Covers trends including AI. Career chapter included.

Very comprehensive with 53 chapters, all in Brazilian Portuguese. Strong logical progression from fundamentals to advanced topics. Excellent coverage breadth: mindset, organizations, teams, Scrum, Kanban, Lean, XP, method selection, products/value, stakeholders, OKRs, backlog, prioritization, planning, refinement, estimation, Scrum events, Kanban practices, metrics, flow management, discovery, dual-track, product techniques, UX, technical practices, quality, DevOps, architecture, distributed architectures, metrics/indicators, experimentation, continuous improvement, risk, stakeholder communication, portfolio, scaling, frameworks at scale, organizational design, value stream management, change management, contracts, compliance, security/privacy, documentation, distributed teams, scope management, economics/finance, UX, data/AI, ethics, anti-patterns, transformation, and career. Good title conciseness overall. Descriptions are direct and informative. Covers some unique topics like 'Arquiteturas distribuídas e trade-offs' and 'Gestão de escopo e trade-offs'. Strong coverage of both soft skills and technical practices. Career chapter is well-placed at the end. Minor issue: some chapters are quite granular (separate chapters for architecture and distributed architectures), but this adds depth. The coverage of security, ethics, documentation, and tools is a nice addition.

Very comprehensive with 49 chapters, all in Brazilian Portuguese. Strong progression from fundamentals to mastery. Covers empiricism, teams/roles, product management, Scrum, Kanban, Lean, XP, method selection, facilitation, coaching, leadership, discovery, dual-track, backlog, user stories, prioritization, planning, DoR/DoD, quality/testing, version control, CI/CD, DevOps, architecture (two chapters), metrics, experimentation, continuous improvement, risk, stakeholders, portfolio, scaling, frameworks, organizational design (Team Topologies), value stream management, change management, contracts, compliance, security/privacy, documentation, distributed teams, scope management, economics, UX, data/AI, ethics, anti-patterns, transformation, and career. Good title conciseness. Descriptions are well-written and direct. Unique strengths: covers security/privacy as a dedicated chapter, documentation and knowledge management, UX integration, data/AI. The 'Diagnóstico de disfunções' chapter is a good addition. Some chapters may overlap slightly (e.g., 'Agile em escala' and 'Frameworks de escala' are quite close). Overall excellent depth and breadth.

Very comprehensive with 50 chapters, all in Brazilian Portuguese. Covers an impressive range including research methods and scientific foundations (unique), statistics/causal inference, ethics, innovation/intrapreneurship, industry-specific cases, and training/corporate education. Strong technical coverage with DevSecOps, SRE, architecture, testing strategies. Good coverage of scaling, portfolio, organizational structure, transformation. Includes career chapter. However, some titles are verbose (e.g., 'Como Iniciar e Escalar sua Carreira em Metodologias Ágeis', 'Integração Contínua, Entrega Contínua e Feature Management', 'Estratégias de Testes Automatizados e Garantia de Qualidade'). Some descriptions could be more concise. The inclusion of 'Métodos de Pesquisa, Evidência Empírica e Fundamentação Científica' and 'Estatística Aplicada, Inferência Causal e Experimentos' is unique and valuable for PhD preparation. The 'Treinamento, Capacitação Corporativa e Difusão de Conhecimento' chapter is a good addition. Has a typo: 'Manifesta Ágil' should be 'Manifesto Ágil'. Some vendor neutrality concerns (SAFe, Spotify in chapter titles within scaling chapter). Minor issue: 'eXtreme Programming' capitalization is unusual.

Very comprehensive with 47 chapters, all in Brazilian Portuguese. Good logical progression. Covers all major frameworks including Shape Up (unique addition). Strong Scrum coverage with dedicated chapters for Product Owner, Scrum Master, DoD/DoR, ceremonies. Good Kanban coverage with three chapters. Includes XP, Lean, Design Thinking, OKRs, scaling (SAFe, LeSS, Nexus, Spotify each with dedicated chapters), transformation, business agility, product discovery, DevOps, compliance, coaching, leadership, psychology, stakeholder management, tools. Career and certifications chapters included. Some titles are slightly verbose (e.g., 'Manifesto Ágil: valores e princípios', 'Mindset ágil vs. mentalidade tradicional'). Names specific vendors/frameworks in titles more than ideal (SAFe, LeSS, Nexus in separate chapter titles). The Spotify Model chapter is a nice addition but could be more vendor-neutral. Good coverage of hardware/physical products (unique). Anti-patterns chapter is well-placed. Missing some depth in areas like financial management, risk management, and ethics compared to top models.

Decent coverage with 23 chapters, all in Brazilian Portuguese. Covers key topics: Manifesto, Lean, Scrum, Kanban, XP, Design Thinking, User Stories, Estimation, Metrics, Facilitation, Coaching, SAFe, LeSS/Nexus, Spotify, OKRs, Business Agility, DevOps, Transformation, Cynefin, Psychological Safety, Contracts, AI, Career. However, significantly fewer chapters than other models means less depth and granularity. Missing important topics: detailed risk management, compliance/governance, financial management, architecture, quality/testing strategies, distributed teams, documentation, ethics, security, portfolio management, value stream management, retrospectives (only briefly mentioned within facilitation). The 'Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe)' title violates vendor neutrality by naming a specific framework as the chapter title. Some titles are a bit verbose (e.g., 'Framework Cynefin e Tomada de Decisão'). The AI chapter is a good modern addition. Overall, while the content covered is accurate and relevant, the lack of depth and missing topics means this wouldn't achieve the 'top 1%' goal as well as the more comprehensive curricula.

Reasonable coverage with 21 chapters, all in Brazilian Portuguese. Covers fundamentals, Cynefin, Scrum, Kanban, XP, Lean, Product Management, Backlog, Prioritization, Estimation, Metrics, Facilitation, Coaching, DevOps, SAFe, alternative scaling frameworks, Business Agility, OKRs, Change Management, Contracts, Career. However, this is the least comprehensive model with only 21 chapters. Missing many important topics: detailed risk management, security/DevSecOps, architecture, quality/testing strategies, distributed teams, documentation, ethics, financial management, detailed retrospectives, user stories (only briefly in backlog), product discovery/dual-track, anti-patterns, tools, leadership in depth, stakeholder management, compliance/governance in depth. The 'Agilidade em Escala: SAFe' title names a specific vendor. Some descriptions could be more detailed. While the topics covered are accurate, the lack of breadth and depth is a significant shortcoming for a curriculum aiming to produce top-tier professionals. Would not adequately prepare someone for certifications or advanced studies.

Model H provides an absolute masterpiece of a curriculum. With 64 chapters, it not only covers all foundational agile frameworks but seamlessly integrates modern Product Management, Site Reliability Engineering (SRE), Team Topologies, Data/Analytics, Hardware/R&D, and organizational psychology. This perfectly aligns with the goal of creating a 'Top 1%' curriculum that prepares learners for highly complex, real-world leadership scenarios. It strictly follows all formatting and negative constraints.

Model C is outstanding and incredibly modern. It covers 51 chapters and includes cutting-edge topics like Team Topologies, Data/AI/MLOps in Agile, and progressive delivery. It creates a highly logical progression from fundamentals to scaling and organizational design. The descriptions and titles are perfectly concise and fluff-free.

Model G is exceptionally deep and uniquely addresses the prompt's requirement of preparing students for 'advanced studies like a master's degree or PhD'. It includes advanced academic and analytical chapters like causal inference, statistical A/B testing, value stream mapping, and empirical research methods. It is comprehensive and strictly follows the formatting rules.

Model E offers a highly structured, comprehensive 55-chapter curriculum. It strikes a great balance between team-level agility, product discovery, technical practices, and organizational leadership. It flawlessly adheres to the concise title/description constraints without any fluff.

Model F provides a very strong and broad 48-chapter curriculum. It stands out by explicitly covering a wide variety of frameworks including Shape Up and the Spotify Model, as well as addressing Agile in hardware and non-software environments. Titles and descriptions are clean and straight to the point.

Model A is a very solid curriculum with 42 chapters. It covers a lot of ground, including older/niche frameworks like Crystal, FDD, and DSDM, giving it great historical breadth. However, it lacks a bit of the modern intersections (like Team Topologies or SRE) seen in the higher-ranked models. It follows all rules perfectly.

Model D provides a good overview of the subject but falls short of the 'Top 1%' and 'comprehensive' requirements. With only 23 chapters, it lacks the depth required to master complex projects, scaling, and advanced technical agility. It followed the formatting constraints well.

Model B is the shortest curriculum with only 21 chapters. While the topics it does cover are relevant, it is far too brief to take a learner from no knowledge to the top 1% of the field. It misses significant depth in product discovery, advanced metrics, scaling complexities, and technical practices.

Currículo muito forte, progressivo e extremamente abrangente, cobrindo fundamentos, Scrum, Kanban, XP, produto, discovery, engenharia, DevOps, SRE, arquitetura, segurança, métricas, escala, portfólio, finanças, compliance, áreas não-TI, trabalho distribuído, IA, ética e carreira. Está em português do Brasil, segue bem a progressão de iniciante a avançado e inclui tendências atuais. Os títulos e descrições são concisos e profissionais. Pequenos pontos de melhoria: alguns títulos ainda ficam um pouco longos e há bastante foco em contexto digital/produto, mas isso é compatível com o tema e não compromete a qualidade geral.

Excelente cobertura e organização pedagógica. Vai dos fundamentos e mindset até produto, backlog, planejamento, discovery, práticas técnicas, DevOps, escala, liderança, mudança organizacional, compliance, arquitetura, ética, tendências e carreira. O conteúdo é moderno, vendor-neutral na maior parte, e muito adequado para formar alguém apto a liderar contextos complexos. Está bem escrito em PT-BR e com boa progressão. Fica ligeiramente atrás do melhor por ter menos profundidade explícita em alguns tópicos avançados como segurança, finanças e contextos regulados, embora toque neles.

Muito abrangente e sofisticado, com excelente cobertura de fundamentos, produto, discovery, qualidade, CI/CD, DevOps, arquitetura, métricas, experimentação, portfólio, escala, Team Topologies, compliance, segurança, IA, ética e transformação. É um dos mais completos para formação avançada. Porém, há um problema importante de aderência linguística: usa muitos termos em inglês desnecessariamente nos títulos e descrições ('Agile', 'Definition of Ready', 'Value Stream Management', etc.), o que enfraquece o requisito de uso de português do Brasil. Ainda assim, o conteúdo é excelente.

Currículo muito completo e bem estruturado, com ótima progressão dos fundamentos até escala organizacional, governança, finanças, risco, business agility, certificações, carreira e tendências. Cobre vários frameworks além de Scrum/Kanban, o que fortalece a proposta de abrangência. Também inclui práticas técnicas, DevOps, discovery e liderança. Perde pontos por alguns títulos e descrições mais longos do que o ideal e por certa verbosidade; além disso, alguns capítulos poderiam ser consolidados enquanto faltam tópicos mais explícitos sobre segurança, UX e fundamentos de complexidade/sistemas.

Cobertura muito ampla, com bom equilíbrio entre fundamentos, produto, engenharia, métricas, experimentação, escala, transformação, pesquisa e carreira. É um dos poucos a incluir explicitamente métodos de pesquisa e base científica, alinhando bem com a exigência de preparação para estudos avançados. No entanto, há alguns problemas: mistura muitos termos em inglês, alguns títulos são longos e pouco diretos, e existe um erro factual/linguístico relevante logo no início ('Manifesta Ágil' em vez de 'Manifesto Ágil'). Também começa a tangenciar bastante áreas adjacentes, o que às vezes dilui o foco do curso.

Bom currículo, amplo e útil, cobrindo bem Scrum, Kanban, XP, Lean, discovery, escalabilidade, transformação, compliance, DevOps, coaching, liderança, certificações e carreira. A progressão é clara e a seleção de tópicos é forte para contexto profissional. Porém, usa muitos nomes específicos e anglicismos, alguns capítulos ficam excessivamente tool/framework-centric, e há lacunas em temas como segurança, ética, finanças, complexidade organizacional e métricas de produto mais avançadas. Ainda é uma resposta sólida.

Resposta boa e coerente, com fundamentos, Lean, Scrum, Kanban, XP, discovery, backlog, métricas, facilitação, coaching, escala, business agility, DevOps, transformação, IA e carreira. Está em português e a sequência é razoável. No entanto, fica claramente menos completa do que as melhores: faltam profundidade em produto, priorização, risco, compliance detalhado, qualidade, arquitetura, portfólio, finanças, trabalho distribuído e tendências mais amplas. É adequada, mas não transmite domínio de topo absoluto do campo.

Estrutura enxuta e clara, com boa base em fundamentos, Scrum, Kanban, XP, Lean, produto, métricas, facilitação, coaching, DevOps, escala, mudança organizacional, contratos e carreira. Porém, está muito aquém da exigência de currículo 'completo' e 'extenso' para levar à maestria: faltam vários tópicos importantes como discovery mais aprofundado, liderança, qualidade, arquitetura, segurança, compliance, gestão de riscos, portfólio, finanças, trabalho remoto, anti-patterns, tendências e aplicações além de TI. É bom como trilha intermediária, não como formação top 1%.
